danger_signals.visual_form: Blocks rising above black threshold line at top of play field
danger_signals.meaning: Imminent game over if any stack crosses the boundary

danger_signals.visual_form: Growing vertical stacks of enemy sprites in columns
danger_signals.meaning: Player is losing control of field state; danger of stack overflow

danger_signals.visual_form: Mario enemy sprites (Goomba, etc.) descending into columns
danger_signals.meaning: Hazards entering play field that must be matched to eliminate

safety_signals.visual_form: Two identical monster blocks colliding and disappearing
safety_signals.meaning: Successful match; stack reduction and points awarded

safety_signals.visual_form: Yoshi eggshell halves joining and a Yoshi character emerging
safety_signals.meaning: Safe zone creation; bonus points; larger Yoshi = more monsters encased

safety_signals.visual_form: Empty columns in B-Type mode after all blocks removed
All stacks remaining below the black boundary marker
safety_signals.meaning: Game state is stable; player has time to strategize

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Mario character sprite at bottom of play field
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Positioned below play field; controls stack swapping mechanics

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Luigi character sprite in multiplayer mode
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Operates separate play field in competitive matches

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Goomba and other Mario series enemy types as falling blocks
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Multiple enemy types visible as distinct block sprites
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Descend from top; represent uncontrolled world state

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Yoshi eggshell halves; hatched Yoshi sprite
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Appear as falling collectible pieces; larger variants signal greater success

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Stacks below threshold line; empty columns visible; minimal block accumulation
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player has time to plan moves; falling monsters arrive at standard rate

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Stacks growing taller; approaching black threshold line; column density increasing
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Block fall speed or spawn rate increases; player must act faster

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Multiple stacks near or at threshold line; play field filling rapidly
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game over imminent; no margin for error; limited strategic options

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Blocks flash/disappear on collision; stacks reduce in height; points appear on screen
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Field pressure momentarily decreases; player gains breathing room

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Eggshell halves join together; Yoshi sprite emerges; size varies by encased monsters
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Large bonus point multiplier applied; psychological reward for successful play

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: All columns empty; play field clear; transition to next level screen
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Progression unlocked; initial block count increases in subsequent level

environmental_storytelling.example: The horizontal line at top of play field serves as visual defeat condition marker; no narrative needed—the line IS the danger
Specific Mario enemy sprites signal which blocks are needed for matching; visual language allows instant puzzle recognition without UI text
Visual tower growth communicates player performance; tall stacks = poor management; shrinking stacks = recovery
Hatched Yoshi sprite size increases with number of monsters encased; visual reward scales with skill/success
Four distinct columns visualize puzzle constraint; player must mentally model state of each column independently

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Move downward continuously; land in assigned column; convert to static block on contact with stack
Bottom half acts like matching block (disappears on contact with identical half); top half seeks bottom half
Hatches into Yoshi; encases all monsters between halves; disappears from field; generates bonus
Swaps adjacent stacks horizontally; responds to player input; position below field indicates control authority
Vanish on collision with falling identical block; trigger cascade if gravity rules apply
Persist in place; accumulate height; contribute to overflow risk

notes: Yoshi (NES, 1992) is a falling-block puzzle game with minimal narrative framing. All world-state communication occurs through block mechanics, visual stacking, and threshold-based danger signaling. The game uses pure abstraction—enemy sprites represent abstract blocks that must match. Yoshi hatching is the sole 'reward' visual distinct from elimination. No faction narrative exists; the game is mechanically rather than narratively driven. Regional title variations (Yoshi no Tamago / Yoshi / Mario & Yoshi) suggest emphasis shifts in localization but do not reflect in-game world-state manifestation. Unused graphics suggest shadow effects were considered for menu clarity but removed.
